claim: Yield stress increases steadily with Al content up to 25 at.% Al, then drops sharply at 25–28 at.% Al, and remains nearly constant or slightly increases from 30 to 40 at.% Al; ductility shows an inverse trend, with a sharp drop up to 25 at.% Al, a slight peak at 28 at.% Al, and a slow decline thereafter.
evidence_text: The data on yield stress ... show a steadily increasing yield stress with Al content up to 25% Al, a rapid fall at 25–28% Al, and little change or a slight increase over the 30–40% Al range. Ductility ... shows an essentially inverse relationship to the yield stress, with a rapid fall in ductility to 25% Al, a slight maximum at 28% Al, and a steady, slow fall thereafter.

claim: In DO3-ordered Fe–Al the perfect superdislocation consists of four (1/2)<111> partials; in B2-ordered material it consists of two (1/2)<111> partials; in disordered or short-range ordered material single (1/2)<111> dislocations are present.
evidence_text: four partial dislocations each of Burgers vector (1/2)〈1 1 1〉 constituting the perfect superdislocation when DO3 order is present; two partial dislocations also of Burgers vector (1/2)〈1 1 1〉 constituting the perfect superdislocation when B2 order is present; single (1/2)〈1 1 1〉 dislocations present in disordered or short range ordered material.

claim: Dipole hardening theory explains the high work hardening rate by the greater strengthening effect of superdislocation dipoles compared to single dislocation dipoles.
evidence_text: The high work hardening rate of the ordered state has been interpreted ... using a dipole hardening mechanism ..., whereby the strengthening effect of the superdislocation dipole is greater than that of the single dislocation dipole.

claim: The APBI fault is the antiphase boundary trailed by a (1/2)<111> dislocation in B2-ordered or DO3-ordered Fe–Al; the APBII fault is trailed by a pair of (1/2)<111> dislocations in DO3-ordered Fe–Al.
evidence_text: The APBI fault is that trailed by a (1/2)〈1 1 1〉 dislocation in material possessing B2 order or in material (of composition near 25–30% Al) possessing additional DO3 order, while the APBII fault is that trailed by a pair of (1/2)〈1 1 1〉 dislocations (in B2 crystal notation) in material possessing DO3 order (obviously of composition near 25–30% Al).

claim: Fe3Al alloys show extremely high elastic anisotropy, with the Zener factor A=2C44/(C11−C12) peaking near the Fe3Al composition due to an increase in C44 and a decrease in (C11−C12)/2.
evidence_text: Fe3Al alloys are seen to have an extremely high degree of elastic anisotropy, as reflected in the Zener anisotropy factor, A=2C44/(C11−C12), where C44, C11, and C12 are stiffness constants. This increased anisotropy factor is seen to be due to both an increase in the value of the C44 parameter and a fall in (C11 – C12)/2.

claim: Alloys were drop cast into a 25 mm diameter cylindrical copper mould giving a grain size of about 0.25 mm; slow cooling after solidification produced a high degree of B2 order for Al‑rich alloys and a good degree of DO3 order for alloys with 25–30% Al, evidenced by strong superlattice spots and coarse DO3 domains in TEM.
evidence_text: produce samples with grain size of the order of 0.25 mm. … Cooling after solidification was sufficiently slow to allow a high degree of B2 order for alloys rich in Al, and a good degree of DO3 order for alloys containing 25–30% Al, as indicated by strong superlattice spots and coarse DO3 domains observed during subsequent transmission electron microscopy.

claim: Compression samples were tested at room temperature to a strain of 2–3% at a nominal strain rate of 3×10⁻⁴ s⁻¹, with the compression axis parallel to the cast cylinder axis; no discrete yield point was observed and the stress–strain curves were always slightly curved.
evidence_text: testing, at room temperature, to a strain of 2–3% at a nominal strain rate of 3×10⁻⁴ s⁻¹. The compression axis was always taken as parallel to the cylinder axis … Compression tests showed a smooth onset of plasticity, with no discrete yield points observed, and the stress–strain behaviour was always slightly curved

claim: In Fe–15% Al deformed 2–3%, single (1/2)⟨111⟩ dislocations form a loose cellular distribution containing many dipole segments (Fig. 5).
evidence_text: Single dislocations arranged in a loose cellular distribution in Fe–15% Al deformed 2–3%. … Many of the dislocation segments in the clustered regions, Fig. 5, were seen to be dipoles.

claim: In Fe–20% Al, deformation proceeds by single curved (1/2)⟨111⟩ dislocations; many bowed dislocations form dipoles, while straight screw segments produce trailing edge dipoles and small dipole loops (Figs. 6 and 7).
evidence_text: Curled single dislocations in Fe–20% Al. … Many of the dislocation segments … were seen to be dipoles … Fig. 7 shows … some straight screw dislocations … together with some narrow edge dipoles and many small dipole loops. These loops and dipoles are seen to be produced by trailing from the gliding screw dislocations.

claim: In Fe–25% Al with DO3 order, dislocations stay straight in near‑screw orientations and form loosely coupled screw dipole pairs and loose superdislocation partials; APB energies are too low to tightly couple the (1/2)⟨111⟩ partials, so they behave as individual dislocations while trailing APBI faults (Figs. 8 and 9).
evidence_text: With the appearance of long range order … dislocations remained more straight … in near-screw orientations and again showed edge dipole segments as well as loosely coupled screw dipole configurations … The APB energies for both APBI and APBII faults were insufficiently high to retain dislocations together as closely coupled superdislocations … they largely glide and behave as individual (1/2)111 dislocations.

claim: In Fe–28% Al, dislocations are invariably superdislocations composed of two (1/2)⟨111⟩ partials that constitute a perfect B2 superdislocation and trail APBII faults without trailing APBI faults; they are straight and lie in near‑screw orientations on {110} planes (Figs. 10 and 11).
evidence_text: dislocations are now invariably superdislocations composed of two (1/2)⟨111⟩ partials—that is they constitute a perfect superdislocation for the B2 lattice, but an imperfect superdislocation for the DO3 lattice, and hence trail APBII faults as they glide. … These superdislocations do not trail any APBI faults … dislocations are generally straight, and are mostly in near-screw orientations.

claim: In Fe–28% Al, straight parallel dislocations with identical ⟨111⟩ Burgers vectors form by reactions between glissile superdislocations on different {110} planes; these produce sessile edge dislocation pairs with ⟨100⟩ Burgers vectors on cube planes, e.g., [111]+[1̄̄11]→ pair of [100] along [010] on (001), which act as locks preventing further room‑temperature superdislocation motion (Figs. 12 and 13).
evidence_text: Imaging the reactions marked r … confirms a reaction of two superdislocations with different ⟨111⟩ Burgers vectors and lying in different {110} glide planes producing a pair of edge dislocations with ⟨100⟩ Burgers vector sitting in a sessile configuration on a cube plane. … a Burgers vector reaction of [1 1 1] + [1 1̄ 1] formed a pair of [1 0 0] edge dislocations along the [0 1 0] direction, hence lying in a (0 0 1) plane. Such configurations constitute locks that prevent further room temperature superdislocation motion.

claim: In Fe–30% Al, deformation proceeds by superdislocations of two (1/2)⟨111⟩ partials with small separation because of high APBI fault energy; straight screw dislocations and edge dipoles/loops dominate, and remnant APBII fringes indicate destruction of weak DO3 order by glide (Figs. 14–16).
evidence_text: Deformation occurs by the movement of the same superdislocations composed of 2 (1/2)⟨111⟩ partial dislocations, … The APBI fault energy is now sufficiently high that the separation of the partials constituting the superdislocation is small … Also visible in Fig. 16 are the large number of fringes … Such fringes are due to residual contrast of the APBII fringes produced during deformation of this weakly DO3 ordered material, since the pairs of (1/2)⟨111⟩ partials destroy this order during movement.

claim: In Fe–35% Al, B2 superdislocations with closely spaced (1/2)⟨111⟩ partials glide; many straight screw superdislocations and edge dipole segments are observed, with dipoles being pulled out from obstacles, and weak remnant APBII fringes attest to destruction of DO3 order (Figs. 17–19).
evidence_text: The dislocations present are superdislocations of the B2 lattice, constituted by two partials of Burgers vector (1/2)⟨111⟩ each, with these partials lying close to each other due to the higher APBI fault energy. … several dipole segments being pulled out of the moving screw superdislocations where these encounter some obstacle. … the presence of some degree of DO3 order means that these superdislocations … destroy some of the DO3 order by their movement, which is indicated by the weak remnant fringes seen in Fig. 19.

claim: In deformed Fe-35% Al alloy, superdislocations of the B2 lattice consist of two (1/2)⟨111⟩ partials lying close to each other due to higher APBI fault energy; many straight near-screw superdislocations and edge dipole segments are observed, with some dipole segments dragged out of moving screw superdislocations encountering obstacles.
evidence_text: The dislocations present are superdislocations of the B2 lattice, constituted by two partials of Burgers vector (1/2)⟨111⟩ each, with these partials lying close to each other due to the higher APBI fault energy. Fig. 17 shows many straight, near-screw superdislocations and edge dipole segments, seen as widely spaced dislocations. Fig. 17 also shows (at right) several dipole segments being pulled out of the moving screw superdislocations where these encounter some obstacle.

claim: A pair of bright field micrographs taken under +g and -g conditions (Fig. 18) reveals a large number of edge dipole segments in deformed Fe-35% Al.
evidence_text: The large number of dipole segments formed are clearly seen in the pair of images shown in Fig. 18 imaged under + / - g conditions.

claim: Weak remnant fringes observed in Fig. 19 indicate that movement of B2 superdislocations (composed of a pair of partials) destroys some of the DO3 order present in Fe-35% Al.
evidence_text: As for the alloys with 28–30% Al, the presence of some degree of DO3 order means that these superdislocations (with a pair of partial dislocations) again destroy some of the DO3 order by their movement, which is indicated by the weak remnant fringes seen in Fig. 19.

claim: Four-partial superdislocations were never observed; for the 25% Al alloy, the APBI energy is so low that dislocations appear completely dissociated as single dislocations trailing an APB fault, and nucleation of four-partial superdislocations near 25–28% Al requires thermal activation at temperatures of a few hundred degrees.
evidence_text: Such four-partial superdislocations have never been observed here, and indeed for the 25% Al alloy the APBI energy is so low that dislocations appear completely dissociated as single dislocations, trailing the corresponding APB fault. For alloys near 25–28% Al, the difficulty of nucleating such four-partial superdislocations is well known, and seems to need thermal activation at temperatures of a few hundred degrees [6,9,10,26,27].

claim: High elastic anisotropy means edge dislocations have much higher energy than screw dislocations, but the relative energy increase for Fe-25% Al on changing a screw dislocation into an edge is only about 5–10% greater than for less anisotropic alloys with 30–34% Al, which seems insufficient to explain the large peak in work hardening rate.
evidence_text: High elastic anisotropy means that edge dislocations will have much greater energy than screw dislocations. However, as seen by analysis of the elastic stiffness data, the change of this increase in elastic energy (as a screw becomes an edge) is relatively small. For example, from Kotter et al. and Yoo et al., the relative energy increase for Fe–25% Al on changing a screw dislocation into an edge is of the order of 1.8–2.7 times, but is only about 5–10% less for the less anisotropic alloys with 30–34% Al. This small difference in energy change as a screw dislocation becomes an edge hardly seems sufficient to change significantly the fraction of screw dislocations present, and thus cause such a large peak in the work hardening rate.

claim: It is alternatively proposed that the high elastic anisotropy at 25–30% Al reflects high Peierls forces, which ensure dislocations are most strongly oriented along screw directions, maximizing the tendency to form dipole pinning points and explaining the work hardening peak near 28% Al.
evidence_text: An alternative possibility to explain the peak in work hardening is to consider that the elastic anisotropy data reflect the changes of interatomic forces which themselves generate correspondingly weaker or stronger Peierls forces on dislocations. Accordingly the high elastic anisotropy (much greater at 25–30% Al) is reflected in higher Peierls forces for these same compositions, especially near 28% Al. These Peierls forces ensure that dislocations here are most strongly oriented along screw directions and the tendency to form dipole pinning points is the strongest.

claim: An additional pinning obstacle for ordered alloys, especially near 28–30% Al, is produced by reaction of ⟨111⟩ dislocations to produce straight edge ⟨100⟩ dislocations, on which Peierls forces are extremely high at room temperature, making them strong barriers to further glide of ⟨111⟩ superdislocations.
evidence_text: An additional pinning obstacle observed for all ordered alloys, but especially for those near 28–30% Al, is that produced by reaction of ⟨111⟩ dislocations to produce straight edge ⟨100⟩ dislocations. Peierls forces are known to be extremely high on such dislocations at room temperature and hence such dislocations are strong barriers to further glide of ⟨111⟩ superdislocations.

claim: Elastic energy calculations based on anisotropic elasticity data suggest that the reaction from a pair of reacting screw (1/2)⟨111⟩{110} dislocations to an edge ⟨100⟩{001} dislocation is not energetically favourable, especially for the most anisotropic 25–30% Al alloys; a slight deviation from screw orientation permits the reaction to be energetically favourable, but Peierls forces rather than elastic anisotropy are suggested to stabilize these obstacles.
evidence_text: Examination of the changes of dislocation elastic energy, from the initial pair of reacting screw (1/2)⟨111⟩{110} dislocations to the final edge ⟨100⟩{001} dislocation, using calculations based on the anisotropic elasticity data would suggest that the reaction is not energetically favourable, and especially not so for the most anisotropic 25–30% Al alloys. A slight deviation of the initial (1/2)⟨111⟩ dislocations from the screw orientation permits the reaction to be energetically favourable, but again there appears to be no energetic reason to justify any preference for reaction for alloy compositions close to the 25–30% Al range. Again, it is suggested that Peierls forces, on both ⟨111⟩ and ⟨100⟩ dislocations may be responsible for the stabilisation of these obstacles, rather than the elastic anisotropy itself.

claim: The small ductility peak observed for Fe–Al alloys near 28% Al is associated with the peak in work hardening rate over the composition range 26–30% Al where the flow stress is relatively constant, consistent with the Considère criterion.
evidence_text: As a final point, we can comment on the small ductility peak observed for alloys with composition near 28% Al, seen in Fig. 1. ... Around 28% Al, the small ductility maximum can be associated with the peak in work hardening rate over a composition range (26–30% Al) where the flow stress is relatively constant.

claim: Dislocation reactions between different ⟨111⟩ dislocations produce sessile ⟨100⟩ edge dislocations in alloys close to the work hardening peak (28–30% Al), which may contribute to the high work hardening.
evidence_text: Dislocation reactions between different ⟨111⟩ dislocations leading to sessile ⟨100⟩ edge dislocations are seen for alloys close to the work hardening peak (28–30% Al) which may also contribute to the high work hardening of such alloys.
